research_answer: Yes, the implied comparative advantage signal adds genuine predictive power even after controlling for all industry-level and location-level fixed effects. When location and industry fixed effects are included — which absorb all average differences between countries and between sectors — the density residual (the gap between implied and actual comparative advantage) remains a highly significant predictor of export growth with a coefficient of -0.024 (p<0.001). The R² rises from 0.412 with fixed effects alone to 0.439 when the density residual is added. Crucially, the density residual is constructed entirely from base-year data, with no forward-looking information, so it's not capturing anything mechanical. This confirms that the product space contains genuine structural information about which specific country-industry combinations are under- or over-shooting their implied equilibrium.
supporting_quotes: Column 8 shows the baseline growth equation with location and industry fixed effects and initial location-industry size. Column 9 reintroduces the density residual and shows that its economic and statistical significance is undiminished. It is important to again point out that the density residual uses only base-year data and thus contains no information regarding future growth, while the coefficients on the fixed effects are calculated ex post.

data_points.metric: R² with fixed effects + density residual
